fields.description: Master kill-switch. When unchecked, no requests or jobs are profiled even if users have active sessions. Use this to pause all instrumentation site-wide without touching individual users' widgets.
fields.label: Profiler Enabled

fields.description: Optimus Session records older than this are eligible for automatic deletion by the daily housekeeping job. Set to 0 to keep forever.
fields.label: Session Retention (days)

fields.options:
Long-running flows (bulk inserts, complex Submit chains) can exceed the per-recording query cap during analyze-time enrichment — each query needs an EXPLAIN round-trip, so very high counts would exceed RQ's job timeout. When capped, the report covers the first N queries and the rest are truncated; a red banner at the top of the report surfaces the truncation so you know the analysis is partial.

Default: 2000 — comfortable for most flows. Raise to 5000 or 10000 for flows that legitimately touch more rows (e.g. Manufacturing Plan Submit creating 100+ child orders). Analyze-time cost scales roughly linearly with this value.

fields.description: Max queries per recording that get enriched (sqlparse format + EXPLAIN + normalization). Anything beyond this is truncated with a warning. Keep reasonable — each query costs one EXPLAIN round-trip.
fields.label: Max Queries per Recording

fields.description: pyinstrument statistical-sampler interval in milliseconds. Lower = finer call-tree resolution but higher overhead. 1ms is the recommended default; raise to 5-10ms for prod-like profiling. Cannot be lower than 0.1ms.
fields.label: Sampler Interval (ms)

fields.description: Drop actions shorter than this many milliseconds from the per-action breakdown in reports. 0 = show everything. Useful for declutter when a flow generates many sub-millisecond background polls.
fields.label: Min Action Duration to Show (ms)

fields.description: Render any duration above this threshold (in milliseconds) as seconds — e.g. with the default of 1000, 5234ms displays as 5.23s but 800ms stays as 800ms. Set to a very large value (e.g. 99999999) to effectively disable.
fields.label: Render durations in seconds above (ms)

fields.description: When checked, the "Time spent per database table" section in the report drops Frappe schema/meta tables (tabDocType, tabDocField, tabSingles, tabPatch Log, tabWorkspace, tabRole, tabDocType Link/Action/State, …), framework-internal tables (tabHas Role, tabDefaultValue, tabUser Social Login, tabUser Role Profile, tabBlock Module, tabUser Email), and information_schema.* — touched by every request via framework machinery, not user-actionable. A small "(N tables hidden)" note replaces them. Other sections (top-queries leaderboard, per-action drill-down, full recordings) are unaffected. Uncheck to see them all.
fields.label: Hide framework / internal database tables in the breakdown

fields.description: How long (seconds, capped at 300) the analyze job waits for the background jobs the profiled flow enqueued to finish before generating the report — so jobs a worker picks up shortly after you click Stop aren't lost. 0 = don't wait (pre-v0.6.0 behavior). On a single-worker bench the analyze job yields the worker between checks so those jobs can actually run; if the scheduler is disabled / no worker is available, the wait is skipped. Captured jobs are analyzed like requests and listed in the report's "Background jobs" section.
fields.label: Wait for Background Jobs (seconds)

fields.options:
Tracked Apps is an allowlist — when populated, only those apps' findings are actionable; everything else routes to Framework-level observations. Leave it empty to keep the default (frappe / erpnext / hrms / lms / helpdesk / insights / crm / builder / wiki / drive / payments and pip libs are treated as framework code).

Ignored Apps is an exclusion list — findings whose blame app falls in this list are dropped from the report entirely (both sections). Use it for apps you can't (or won't) patch (typically frappe, optimus, sometimes erpnext). The two compose: an app in Ignored is dropped before bucketing, regardless of Tracked.

Do not add frappe or erpnext to Tracked Apps. Doing so flips them to "user code" and floods your actionable findings with framework noise — the opposite of what most users want. If you want them gone, add them to Ignored Apps instead.

fields.description: Findings whose blame app falls in this list are dropped from the report entirely — both "Findings — what to fix" and "Framework-level observations". Use it for apps you can't or won't patch (typically frappe, optimus, sometimes erpnext). Default empty (nothing dropped). The "Issues found" stat card shows the kept count + a small note ("N findings hidden from ignored apps: …") so the total stays honest.
fields.label: Ignored Apps

fields.options: How many identical calls from the same callsite qualify as a Redundant Call finding, and how many identical queries from the same callsite qualify as an N+1 finding. Lower = more sensitive = more findings; higher = fewer findings but less noise.

fields.description: Minimum get_doc calls for the same (doctype, name) from the same callsite before a Redundant Call finding is emitted.
fields.label: Redundant get_doc Threshold

fields.description: Minimum cache lookups for the same key from the same callsite before a Redundant Call finding is emitted. Cache lookups aren't individually timed, so low-count loops are indistinguishable from framework background noise — 50 matches the high-severity threshold and cuts 0ms noise.
fields.label: Redundant Cache Lookup Threshold

fields.description: Minimum has_permission calls for the same (doctype, name, ptype) from the same callsite before a Redundant Call finding is emitted.
fields.label: Redundant Permission Check Threshold

fields.description: Minimum identical queries from the same callsite before an N+1 Query finding is emitted.
fields.label: N+1 Minimum Occurrences

fields.options: Tune what counts as a high-severity finding. Sites with intentionally slow analytical flows (legitimate full-table scans, large reports) want higher numbers to suppress false positives; sites with strict latency budgets want lower numbers to surface anything borderline.

fields.description: Single SQL query duration above this is flagged as a Slow Query finding. Default 200ms — anything below is usually within acceptable per-query budget.
fields.label: Slow Query Threshold (ms)

fields.description: Slow Hot Path threshold as a percentage of the action's wall time. A subtree consuming >= this % AND >= the min-ms below qualifies. Default 25% with 200ms minimum.
fields.label: Slow Hot Path Threshold (% of action wall time)

fields.description: Slow Hot Path minimum absolute time. A subtree needs both >= the % above AND >= this many ms to qualify.
fields.label: Slow Hot Path Minimum (ms)

fields.description: Phase 2 Hot Line: a single line consuming this %% of the function's total time AND >= the min-ms below is flagged High severity. Default 50% / 100ms.
fields.label: Hot Line High Severity (% of function time)

fields.description: Phase 2 Hot Line minimum absolute time for High severity.
fields.label: Hot Line High Severity Min (ms)

fields.options: Defaults for the phase-2 line-profile pass. The picker dialog still lets the user override these per run.

fields.description: Cap on how many phase-2 runs can be retained per Optimus Session. When exceeded, the oldest run's full results are archived to a private File and only the row metadata is kept in the DocType.
fields.label: Phase 2: Max Runs per Session

fields.description: When checked, the picker dialog's 'Auto-expand hot chain' checkbox starts ticked. Auto-expansion walks each curated pick down phase-1's call tree to instrument the full hot chain in one shot.
fields.label: Phase 2: Default Auto-Expand Hot Chain

fields.description: Maximum levels deep that auto-expand will follow into the call tree from a curated pick.
fields.label: Auto-Expand: Max Depth

fields.description: Auto-expand stops descending when the next-hottest child consumes less than this many milliseconds. Lower = follow deeper into smaller hot spots; higher = stop earlier and instrument fewer functions.
fields.label: Auto-Expand: Minimum Child Time (ms)

fields.options: Patterns and users to exclude from instrumentation. Matches against request path / job kwargs / session user. The profiler's own admin endpoints are always skipped — these settings extend the built-in skip list.

fields.description: One pattern per line. URL paths starting with any of these prefixes are NOT profiled, even when an active session exists. Useful for healthcheck endpoints, polling APIs, etc. Lines starting with # are treated as comments. Example: /api/method/ping
fields.label: Skip Request Paths

fields.description: One user email per line. Requests / jobs running as one of these users are NOT profiled. Useful for excluding system bot users (scheduler, health-checks). Lines starting with # are treated as comments.
fields.label: Skip Users

fields.options: When enabled, a Suggest a fix (AI) button appears on the Optimus Session form. Clicking it sends the selected finding's context — finding type, callsite, a window of surrounding source code, the normalized SQL and EXPLAIN row — to the configured LLM and stores the returned fix on the finding (it also shows up in the regenerated HTML report). This sends code and queries to your chosen provider. Point Base URL at a local model (Ollama / LM Studio / vLLM) to keep everything on-box.

fields.description: Master switch for the AI fix-suggestion feature. Off by default.
fields.label: Enable AI Fix Suggestions

fields.description: Wire format / hosted defaults. "OpenAI-compatible" requires a Base URL and Model — use it for Ollama, LM Studio, vLLM, OpenRouter, Together, Groq, etc.
fields.label: Provider

fields.description: Leave blank to use the hosted default for the chosen provider. Required for "OpenAI-compatible" — e.g. http://localhost:11434/v1 (Ollama), http://localhost:1234/v1 (LM Studio).
fields.label: Base URL

fields.description: Leave blank to use the provider default. Examples: claude-sonnet-4-6 (Anthropic), gpt-4.1-mini (OpenAI), kimi-k2-0905-preview (Kimi), or your local model name.
fields.label: Model

fields.description: API key for the provider, if it needs one. Stored encrypted per-site. Most local OpenAI-compatible endpoints (Ollama, LM Studio, vLLM) don't need a key; hosted ones (OpenAI, Anthropic, Kimi, OpenRouter, Together, Groq) do.
fields.label: API Key

fields.description: Pick which sections of the report may use the LLM. Turning one off is a hard disable — that section is never auto-generated, its buttons on the Optimus Session form are hidden, the API refuses, and re-rendered reports omit it.
fields.label: Use the LLM for

fields.description: When off: no AI-suggested fix on findings — not auto-generated during analyze, the "Suggest a fix (AI)" / "Generate AI fixes" / "Re-evaluate AI fixes" buttons are hidden, the API refuses, and re-rendered reports omit the AI-fix block.
fields.label: Fix suggestions on findings

fields.description: When off: no "Index advice (AI)" block in the "Time spent per database table" section — not auto-generated, the "Suggest an index (AI)" button is hidden, the API refuses, and re-rendered reports omit it.
fields.label: Index recommendations (DB-tables breakdown)

fields.description: When checked, the auto-generated "Steps to Reproduce" note on each session is rewritten by the LLM into a friendly, human-readable flow (the raw captured-actions list is kept below it; falls back to it if the LLM call fails). When off: the note stays the plain action list, the "Humanize Steps (AI)" button is hidden, and the API refuses. (An already-humanized older session keeps its note until re-analyzed.)
fields.label: Humanized "Steps to Reproduce"

fields.description: When checked, the analyze pipeline asks the LLM for a fix for each eligible finding automatically, so suggestions are already in the report — no need to click "Suggest a fix (AI)" per finding. Costs LLM tokens (and a little extra analyze time) on every session that's analyzed; keep it off unless you want suggestions baked in by default.
fields.label: Suggest AI fixes in the report by default

fields.description: Cap on how many findings get an automatic suggestion per session — the highest-severity, highest-impact ones first. 0 = every eligible finding (can be slow + costly on big sessions).
fields.label: Max auto-suggested findings per session
